title: Chapter 6 Quiz: Process Domination
description: Test your knowledge of process management, signals, and job control.

questions.question: What is the difference between a program and a process?
options: A program is running; a process is stored on disk.
A program is stored on disk; a process is a running instance in memory.
A program is a daemon; a process is a script.
questions.explanation: A program is static code on disk; a process is the executing instance.

questions.question: The first user-space process started by the kernel (init/systemd) always has PID 1.
questions.explanation: True. PID 1 is reserved for the init process.

questions.question: Which command displays a real-time, updating view of processes?
questions.explanation: `top` provides a live, updating display.

questions.question: To run a command in the background immediately upon starting it, you append the character `_____`.
questions.explanation: Appending `&` runs the command in the background.

questions.question: What is the default signal sent by the `kill` command?
questions.explanation: `kill` defaults to SIGTERM (15), which politely asks the process to terminate.

questions.question: A process in state 'Z' (Zombie) is actively consuming CPU and RAM.
questions.explanation: False. Zombies have released all resources but retain a process table entry waiting for the parent to reap them.

questions.question: Inside `top`, which key sorts processes by CPU usage?
questions.explanation: Uppercase `P` sorts by CPU.

questions.question: You need to force-kill a process that is ignoring SIGTERM. Which signal number is SIGKILL?
questions.explanation: SIGKILL is signal 9.

questions.question: What does `kill -HUP 1234` typically do to a well-behaved daemon?
questions.explanation: Daemons often interpret SIGHUP as a request to reload configuration without restarting.

questions.question: Which command finds the PID of 'firefox'?
questions.explanation: `pidof` returns the PID of a running program by name.

questions.question: You have a suspended job (Stopped). Which command resumes it in the background?
questions.explanation: `bg` resumes a suspended job in the background.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `nohup` command?
options: Prevents a process from being killed by SIGHUP when the terminal closes.
Stops a process from using the CPU.
Runs a process with maximum priority.
questions.explanation: `nohup` makes a process immune to the HUP signal, allowing it to survive terminal closure.

questions.question: What does `ps aux | grep [f]irefox` accomplish that `ps aux | grep firefox` does not?
options: It excludes the grep command itself from the output.
It shows the parent process ID.
questions.explanation: The bracket trick matches 'firefox' but `grep` sees `[f]irefox`, so it doesn't match its own command line.

questions.question: You press `Ctrl+Z` while a command is running. What happens?
options: It is suspended (stopped) and placed in the background.
It continues running in the background.
It is sent to the printer.
questions.explanation: Ctrl+Z sends SIGTSTP, suspending the foreground process.

questions.question: `pkill` can kill processes based on the username that owns them.
questions.explanation: True. `pkill -u username` kills all processes owned by that user.

questions.question: What does the `disown` command do?
options: Changes the owner of a process.
Removes a job from the shell's job table.
questions.explanation: `disown` removes the job from the shell's table, preventing SIGHUP from being sent when the shell exits.

questions.question: A process is stuck in State 'D'. What is the only way to remove it?
questions.explanation: State D (Uninterruptible Sleep) processes are stuck in kernel code, usually waiting for I/O. They cannot be killed by userspace signals.

questions.question: Which key quits the `top` program?
questions.explanation: Press `q` to quit `top`.

questions.question: You want to see the process hierarchy (tree) using `ps`. Which option provides this?
questions.explanation: `ps aux --forest` displays parent-child relationships visually.

questions.question: Which terminal multiplexer is a modern alternative to `screen`?
questions.explanation: `tmux` is a powerful terminal multiplexer that allows session detachment and reattachment.
